sentences.text: I'm Feigi Fania Stollar Gluss.
Yes.
F-A-I-N-A, Fania.
Feigi, F-I-G-I-E.
Stollar, S-T-O-L-L-A-R.
Gluss, G-L-U-S-S.
I was born in '26, 1926, January the 30th, 3-0, 30th.
[INAUDIBLE]
I was born in Tuczyn, Poland-- Poland.
Tuczyn, T-U-C-Z-Y-N, Tuczyn.
It was not a big city, but we lived among Ukrainian and Polacks.
I can't remember actually, but it was quite a few thousand.
I'm sorry, I don't know.
It was a very nice community.
We had shuls.
We had a kindergarten.
We had schools.
And it was a very, very nice-- we had organizations, Jewish organizations, like Betar, [NON-ENGLISH], even Mizrachi.
So we had quite a few organizations.
Yes, Betar,
This was organization that they didn't believe that with money we can buy Israel.
This organization, we knew that we have to be strong to have our own land.
I was a very happy child.
I grew up with a very loving mother.
She was loving and caring, and she was wonderful.
And she gave me more than material things.
She gave me lot-- lots of love.
And I had very nice friends and neighbors, and we cared for each other.
The neighbors-- we were just like one family with neighbors.
And I had uncles and aunts.
It was a wonderful-- I have very good, nice memories from my-- from my child-- since I was a child.
Very, very nice.
My brother.
I had a brother.
He was one year older than me.
We were like a twin.
His name was Mendl.
I had a lot of friends.
We were a class of 35 children.
Yeah, mixed.
It was a public school.
But it was a school-- we had a school with only Jewish children.
Yes, we wore uniforms, pleated navy skirts and sailor blouses.
The lady who made the uniforms is now in Montreal.
Her name is [INAUDIBLE].
She was our neighbor.
Handmade.
A few bought-- my auntie was in Warsaw.
So She used to buy for me.
They used to dress me up very, very nice.
I used to dress very, very nice.
People used to think that I'm the best dressed girl.
I was still a little girl in--
I can't desc-- How to describe the holidays?
With family, with aunts, uncles, and it was very joyful and very happy.
I grew up next to a shul, because my-- I didn't have-- my grandfather was in the States.
I had my grandfather, and then one grandfather died.
But it's a relative of my mother's that didn't have children, so my mother was like his daughter.
So I was like his grandchild, and I used to call him [?
Zeida ?].
He had a shul, and I grew up in this shul.
My father was in Cuba.
Because my grandfather, my mother's father, was in the United States.
And we couldn't go to the United States, so they tried to get my father to Cuba.
And for a husband, was easier to bring the family.
As long as we would be in Cuba, it would be possible to go to the United States.
Unfortunately, my father got sick and he died there.
I don't remember.
I was-- I-- I was six months when he left.
I don't remember my father.
And my brother didn't remember my father.
I was a very cheerful little girl.
Everybody liked me.
They used to tell my mother that I'm like a gypsy, I go to everybody.
I loved people.
I always loved people.
It was terrible.
Horrible.
But I remember, when I-- when I started to understand, I knew that I'm a dirty Jew, and my place is in Palestine.
It was really like a handicap.
From Ukrainian and Polacks.
Both Ukrainian and Polacks.
From children, because the children, they were told by the adults.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a very horrible place where I grew up.
Danger, we always felt.
But in '39, when the war started, German-- the German-- the war with Poland, Germany and Poland, the Germany and Poland war-- this was in '39.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, the Russian occupied our-- our-- how do you say province?
Because the Russian Germany divided, at that time, Poland.
So we were on the Russian side until '41.
When the war started, I was 12.
I was home.
I was home.
I was with my mother, and brother, and with the rest of the family then.
It changed completely, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because I wasn't-- this was already a Russian school, so it was difficult to start.
I was-- till 12, I was in a Polish school, and then in a Russian school.
But it was OK for two years, till the war started again.
Till '41, from '39 till '41, it was OK.
I went to a Russian school, and I was still a very happy child.
A ghetto?
It took a little while [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because I want to tell you the morning when the Germans-- when I saw the first army, the German faces.
This was in '41.
I don't-- I don't remember exactly the month.
Maybe it was September, or the end of August.
So the army marched in.
And the first, just to look to that-- to look at them, you didn't need a bullet to be killed, with their faces and with their anger.
You feel that you're already killed.
And they-- the first thing they did, they took my mother and a few other of neighbors that I still remember their names, to burn the Sefer Torahs.
Yeah.
Esther Belinski, and Feiga Linnick, and Zisel Sprintz, and my mother.
And they had to burn the Sefer Torahs.
But the Sefer Torahs were very hard to burn, so they beat them up.
And this was-- I had to watch this, and it-- this will remain for the rest of my life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were all wearing belts, and on every belt the soldiers was [NON-ENGLISH].
This means that God was with them.
This was the first impression they made on me, the first morning.
It was the most beautiful sunny morning, but for us was the end.
We had to have-- we had to wear, in the beginning, white [INAUDIBLE].
We have to wear with the [INAUDIBLE].
This was only for a few weeks till we wore the yellow-- the yellow-- how do you call them?
We called them [NON-ENGLISH], the yellow.
Yeah, so I remember my mother, from the backs of the pillow-- little pillows that were yellow-- she made all them out of [NON-ENGLISH].
How do you call them, the yellow?
How do you really call them?
The yellow [INAUDIBLE], we called them.
Patch.
The yellow patch.
Yeah.
But the Ukrainian, they made a pogrom.
This is-- you know what a pogrom is?
OK.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't know, in the middle of the night, they were killing people without bullets, so we didn't know until the morning.
And in the morning, half of our town was dead.
This was already too much to take for the nerves.
People were are already in such a shape, that it was terrible even to talk to one on another.
No, because the Ukrainian got drunk, and they decided they make a-- to massacre the Jews.
And that's what they started to do in the evening, and they finished in the morning.
And they did-- no one did know about the other.
They didn't kill them with-- with bullets, and that's why we couldn't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They cut them.
They did it with ax, with-- with knives.
It was just horrible to see the corpse on the wagons to bury them.
It was the most horrible thing.
And this wasn't all.
They took the intelligents.
They took, like, 16 people, the doctors and the intelligents.
And we lived not far from the police.
And it was a Friday, and my mother was lighting the candles.
And every shot by the Germans, the SS did it.
They shot first the 16-- the 16, the intelligents.
And then, they didn't still make the ghetto.
They took us from one part of the city.
They wanted to have us together, so they took from one part and they put us in another part we should be together.
And before the killings then, they made the ghetto.
The ghetto is organized with [SIGHS] fences, very high fences that you can't go out and you can't go in.
And the-- they're watching you always, the police-- the Ukrainian police, not the-- not the Germans.
They gave the job to the Ukrainian police.
There were-- there were people who worked.
They took still people out to work, by the police.
So there were people that they paid them, like, potatoes or flour.
Or people used to change.
It was our time to change, if you had a good coat [INAUDIBLE] so you changed for food, for potatoes, or-- it wasn't enough food.
Yeah, there was a Judenrat.
This-- there was a Judenrat, and my uncle was in the Judenrat.
For instance, there was the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a name for them was [NON-ENGLISH], because there was [NON-ENGLISH], and there was SS.
The [NON-ENGLISH] used to-- they arrived to our city demanding all the leather things, and silver, gold.
And this committee made sure that all of us gave into this place, and they picked it up from there.
The SS, no.
This was the-- this was the job from the [NON-ENGLISH].
SS was the killers.
We knew what's going on because they knew.
The-- the-- my uncle, we knew from our uncle because he was in the committee.
And he gave everybody [? neft ?].
You know [? neft ?], yeah?
They called it-- I don't know, [? neft ?].
And he told the Jewish people, and I'll go out, and they'll shoot me.
This was two days after Yom Kippur.
There were three graves already prepared.
We knew there were three graves.
And it wasn't far from the place I lived.
So when he was shot, the Jews started to burn the ghetto.
We did it because we lived not far-- the ghetto was not far from a cemetery, and not far from a forest.
The forest, the name was [?
Postamit ?].
You know?
And he wanted at least somebody should be able to run out and to live through the war, and to tell the horrible things what's happening to us.
So people got burnt to death, and the next day, they took them in wagons to bury them-- the ashes.
The Ukrainian took the ashes.
But they took-- but they took the leaders from the community out?
The-- yes.
Oh, sure.
They took the leaders, definitely.
The leaders were the first ones to go.
We already didn't celebrate.
We knew what's coming.
We knew already.
Because we were locked in, and we knew there was graves.
People talked, you know.
We knew there was three graves for us, so we knew.
And this was-- and in '42, Yom Kippur was on a Monday-- that I'll never forget-- and two days after, on Wednesday, was the killing.
They made [NON-ENGLISH].
This means no Jews anymore.
I was in the ghetto, and I was outside the ghetto because I was a shepherd.
And I was [INTERPOSING VOICES] with a very nice couple a few kilometers, not far from our-- from our city.
And this was a couple-- they didn't have children, and they liked me very much.
And he was a native, a German native, but he was the most wonderful human being.
Heinrich Elko.
And his wife was a Polish, Baginska.
She came from a very-- and they really wanted me as their daughter, because I was a very friendly-- very friendly, and I didn't look Jewish at all.
Even Jewish people couldn't tell that I'm Jewish.
So I used to commute.
I used to be there, and them to come to the ghetto to see my mother, see my brother, and see the rest of them, and then I used to go back.
You had permission to leave the ghetto to work for them?
Yeah.
They took me out.
They used to come and take me.
They used to bring me, the-- this couple.
I stayed from '4-- from '42 I think till '43.
It's very hard for me to remember the dates.
I stayed with them, and then I had to leave them because the Ukrainians started to kill the Polacks.
So they had to run.
They had to run [? himself ?].
So that time I met up with our neighbors, a family Salzman-- a mother, a son, and two daughters.
One of them was my close friend, a school friend.
What was her name?
What was her first name?
Chaya Salzman and Shayntzeh Salzman.
They are both in Israel.
But the mother-- but the mother and-- and brother, they went to look for bread for us, and they killed them.
So we never saw them again.
You see, when you were running, you meet all kinds.
When you run, you met all kind, in the forest, people.
There was a forest.
And you meet people running, and you knew they were Jews, you understand?
So all of a sudden I saw [?
Heika ?] and I saw Shayntzeh and I saw-- you know, your mother and brother, Yossel, his name.
He was the only son.
They were very close family.
Because where-- where [?
Elko ?] lived, the German, where I-- where I stayed with them, it was only right next to the forest.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were surrounded by forests.
There was a lot of Jews in the forest, but they killed them.
The Ukrainians killed them.
There were a few thousand. 2,000 were killed in this forest in [?
Postamit ?], not by the Germans, but the Ukrainian.
I was afraid of everybody except not of dogs.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Of Jews, no.
No.
This is the family what I told you, the Salzman.
And then Natan Schulman is in Israel.
He saved me from rape.
What can I say?
That he saved me.
He really-- he really saved me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from-- from maybe four or five of them.
I see him when I go to Israel.
I used to travel, and we are very close.
He was a neighbor of ours.
I knew him since I was a little girl.
In the middle of the night, the men used to go begging.
Or they used to go in the fields, when-- in the-- when, for instance, when there were still potatoes, like the-- in September-- September, October, when there are still potatoes in the ground.
They used to-- they used to get from potatoes.
And then they used to beg for food.
There were some of them, they used to give food.
Some of them.
Some of them, because, for instance, everybody, they knew somebody.
They dealt with them.
So they knew the better ones, so they gave them food.
They gave them milk or bread.
And then, the used to-- the-- the Ukrainian, they used to kill cows.
So when the-- the dirt from the cows, they used to-- we used to cook it.
Can you imagine?
And none of us were sick.
None of us got sick stomachs.
And they shared all this with you?
We shared, all of us.
No clothing.
We were in rags.
That was the clothing.
How we lived?
That's why I ask myself the same question.
Because you see, the Jews, they were so capable.
They made bunkers in the ground that you couldn't even see the bunkers.
You could never believe it.
And that-- and we were in those bunkers in the cold, in the dampness.
But we were lying one-- one next to the other, to keep us warm.
I can't-- you know, I can't even think now.
What did I have?
The Ukrainian, they have from wood.
I don't know how you call them.
We called them [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know how you call them in English.
You know?
Wood.
They make, from wood, shoes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Wood shoes.
In the bunkers.
Winter was very bad because the Ukrainian could find us very easy because of the snow.
They could trace us.
And the summer was easier because they put grass on the bunkers.
So they couldn't-- they made such a wonders, I'm telling you.
You couldn't even see there are people hiding inside in this-- in this darkness.
We used to think we'll survive another hour, another minute, and we'll be killed.
That's what we spend our time thinking, and praying only to be shot, not to be tortured.
No, no such a thing.
No.
No.
We were so frightened and so much fear, always.
Always very bad palpitations, scared.
It's a terrible thing, fear.
You want to live, and you know you'll die.
Yes, some of them, they-- they left.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I buried a lot of people.
With my hands I buried with them, with shovels, because they were killed and they weren't buried.
So the only parts, the remains.
And when I think about it, how I could do it, I still it's a mystery to me.
And I-- I don't think I'll ever find that out how I did it.
I never cried.
Couldn't cry.
There was no tears in my eyes.
Couldn't cry.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- and I recognized people that were close to me, dead.
They were dead.
Like our-- our neighbor, Salzman again, Salzman.
And a little girl.
It was the head of the little girl in the sweater, the angora pink sweater, that I could never forget.
I can't even-- I can't even tell you the names of the people that I buried.
And the most, was the most shocking, that my friends, the Salzmans, they had a sister, and she run away with-- she was already married with a little boy.
And it was a chubby little boy, and I loved him.
I don't remember.
He wasn't a year old.
He was a very chubby little boy, and I used to like to play with him.
So when-- this was November.
This I'll never forget, November the 11th.
Why, because we had at that time the first snow in '42.
